1.title: A voice from the tent
1.text: The book opens with the Lord calling Moses and speaking out of the tabernacle of the congregation. Unlike Sinai with its fire and thunder, here God instructs His people from the sanctuary just built in the midst of the camp.

2.text: Unlike the burnt sacrifice of the previous chapter, the meat offering had no blood: it was fine flour, oil, and frankincense, linking the people's farm labor to their worship.

11.title: Two signs together
11.text: To be clean an animal had to meet BOTH requirements at once: chew the cud AND part the hoof. That is why the camel, the coney, the hare, and the swine were excluded — each failed one of the two signs.

12.title: The offering of the poor
12.text: Verse 8 lets a woman who could not bring a lamb offer two birds instead. This humbler offering is the one Joseph and Mary presented when they brought the infant Jesus to the temple (Luke 2:24), a sign of the family's modest means.

13.title: The priest as examiner
13.text: Under the law of Moses it was the priest, not a physician, who inspected the skin and pronounced a person “clean” or “unclean”; the condition addressed here was first of all ritual and communal, not merely medical.

20.title: Molech and child sacrifice
20.text: Molech was a Canaanite god to whom children were offered in fire. To "give of his seed" meant handing one's own children over to that cult, which the Lord declares defiles his sanctuary and profanes his holy name.

23.title: Israel's sacred calendar
23.text: Leviticus 23 gathers in one place all of Israel's yearly feasts — Passover, Unleavened Bread, Firstfruits, Pentecost, Trumpets, Atonement and Tabernacles — called 'holy convocations'.
